sessions.content:
## The RADIO Framework

System design interviews are not free-form architecture discussions. They are structured assessments with a clear rubric. The single most effective thing you can do is adopt a consistent, named framework and execute it reliably. The RADIO framework does exactly that.

### What is RADIO?

RADIO is a 5-phase structure for system design interviews:

| Phase | Name | Purpose | Time |
|-------|------|---------|------|
| R | **R**equirements | Understand what to build and at what scale | 5 min |
| A | **A**PI Design | Define the contract between client and server | 5 min |
| D | **D**ata Model | Choose storage and define key schemas | 5 min |
| I | **I**nfrastructure | Draw and narrate the core architecture | 20 min |
| O | **O**ptimization | Address bottlenecks and trade-offs | 10 min |

Total: 45 minutes.

### Why Structure Matters

Without a framework, candidates typically:
1. Spend 20 minutes on requirements and run out of time for the design
2. Jump immediately to infrastructure and forget to ask about scale
3. Cover topics in random order, which makes them look disorganised

Interviewers score structure explicitly. At companies like Meta, Google, and Amazon, "structured communication" is a named evaluation dimension.

### The Framework in Practice

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[R: Requirements\n5 min] --> A[A: API Design\n5 min]
    A --> D[D: Data Model\n5 min]
    D --> I[I: Infrastructure\n20 min]
    I --> O[O: Optimization\n10 min]
```

### Phase R — Requirements (5 minutes)

Goal: agree on what you are building and at what scale.

Ask:
- "What are the top 3 things a user can do in this system?"
- "What scale should I design for — how many daily active users?"
- "Is there a latency or availability SLA I should know about?"
- "What is explicitly out of scope for this interview?"

Once you have answers, summarise them back: "So I'll design a system that handles X, Y, Z for 100M DAU with p99 latency under 200ms. I'll set aside features A and B. Does that sound right?"

### Phase A — API Design (5 minutes)

Goal: define the interface your system exposes.

Design 3–5 endpoints that cover the core use cases. Use REST or gRPC as appropriate. Example for a URL shortener:
```
POST /urls          → creates a short URL
GET  /{shortCode}   → redirects to original URL
DELETE /urls/{id}   → deletes a URL
GET  /urls/{id}/stats → returns click analytics
```

This phase forces you to think like a product engineer, not just a systems engineer.

### Phase D — Data Model (5 minutes)

Goal: choose your database(s) and sketch key schemas.

- Which database type fits (SQL, NoSQL, graph, time-series)?
- What are the 2–3 core tables/collections?
- What are the primary keys and foreign keys?
- What indexes do you need?

### Phase I — Infrastructure (20 minutes)

Goal: draw the system architecture and walk through the happy path end-to-end.

Draw: clients → load balancer → services → databases/caches/queues → CDN/storage. Walk through at least 2 core flows (e.g., write path and read path).

### Phase O — Optimization (10 minutes)

Goal: identify and address bottlenecks.

Ask yourself: "What breaks first when traffic 10×s?" Then propose targeted solutions: caching, sharding, CDN, message queues, rate limiting.

### Interview Dialogue: Opening

> Interviewer: "Design Twitter."
>
> You: "I'd love to. Let me start by clarifying requirements before I jump into design — is that okay? [Pause for yes] Great. Could you tell me which features are in scope? Are we building the full Twitter or focusing on a subset like the timeline and tweet posting? [Wait for answer] And what scale should I target — roughly how many daily active users? [Wait for answer] Okay, so 300M DAU, core features are posting tweets, following users, and reading a timeline. Anything explicitly out of scope — like DMs or search? [Wait] Perfect. Let me write those down and then we'll talk through the API."

This opening alone signals maturity. Most junior candidates skip straight to "so I'd use a load balancer..."

### Common Mistakes

1. **No framework at all**. Random topic order wastes time and confuses the interviewer.
2. **Over-indexing on one phase**. 30 minutes on requirements and 10 minutes for the whole architecture is a common failure pattern.
3. **Not summarising back**. Never assume you understood — repeat the requirements back and get confirmation.
keyTakeaways: RADIO: Requirements, API, Data, Infrastructure, Optimization — 5/5/5/20/10 minute split
State the framework at the start: 'I'll use a structured approach, starting with requirements'
Always summarise requirements back and get confirmation before moving on
Interviewers score structure as a named dimension — it is not just a soft skill

sessions.title: How to Clarify Requirements
sessions.paretoJustification: Poor requirements lead to designing the wrong system. Clarifying well is the highest-leverage 5 minutes of the interview.
objectives: Distinguish functional requirements from non-functional requirements
Ask targeted scale questions that produce numbers you can use
Identify and explicitly exclude out-of-scope features

activities.description: Study the functional vs non-functional requirements taxonomy with examples
Practice requirements clarification on 5 different problems (Twitter, Uber, YouTube, WhatsApp, Dropbox)
Write out the requirements summary statement for each problem in 3 sentences

reviewQuestions: Give 3 examples of functional requirements and 3 examples of non-functional requirements for a ride-sharing app.
How do you extract a DAU number from an interviewer who says 'design for large scale'?
Why is it important to name what is OUT of scope?
sessions.successCriteria: Can extract and document complete functional and non-functional requirements in 5 minutes for any given problem.
sessions.content:
## How to Clarify Requirements

Requirements clarification is the phase most candidates rush through. They hear "design Twitter" and immediately start talking about databases. This is a trap. The requirements phase determines whether you build the right system — and interviewers penalise candidates who design confidently in the wrong direction.

### Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements

**Functional requirements**: what the system does — the user-visible behaviours.
- "Users can post tweets up to 280 characters"
- "Users can follow other users"
- "Users can see a timeline of tweets from accounts they follow"

**Non-functional requirements**: how the system does it — quality attributes.
- "The system should support 300M DAU"
- "Timeline reads should have p99 latency under 200ms"
- "The system should be available 99.99% of the time"
- "Tweets should never be lost once posted (durability)"

Both matter. A system that has all the features but goes down 5% of the time is not acceptable.

### The Requirements Question Playbook

Memorise this sequence of questions:

**1. Scope questions (functional)**:
- "What are the most important features for this design?"
- "Are we building read functionality, write functionality, or both?"
- "What is explicitly out of scope for today?"

**2. Scale questions (non-functional)**:
- "How many daily active users should I design for?"
- "What is the expected read-to-write ratio?"
- "Is there a peak traffic window I should be aware of?"

**3. Quality attribute questions (non-functional)**:
- "What is the latency target? Is sub-second response acceptable or do we need 50ms?"
- "What is the availability requirement — 99.9% or 99.99%?"
- "Is strong consistency required, or is eventual consistency acceptable?"
- "Can we tolerate data loss in failure scenarios?"

### Extracting Numbers from Vague Answers

Some interviewers will say "design for millions of users" or "large scale." Do not accept this — you need a number to estimate.

> Interviewer: "Design for large scale."
>
> You: "To make sure I size the system correctly, could you give me an order of magnitude? Are we talking 10 million DAU like a mid-sized app, or 100 million like a regional leader, or 1 billion like a global platform?"

The interviewer will pick one. Now you have a number.

### The Consistency vs Availability Trade-off Question

This is the most important non-functional question for distributed systems. Ask it explicitly:

> "If there's a network partition and some nodes can't communicate, should the system stay available (serve potentially stale data) or stay consistent (refuse to serve until the partition heals)?"

For social feeds: eventual consistency is fine (seeing a tweet 1 second late is okay).
For banking: strong consistency is required (balance must be correct).

### Naming Out-of-Scope Features

Always explicitly name what you are NOT building:

> "Just to confirm scope — I'll focus on posting tweets and reading timelines. I'm setting aside DMs, search, notifications, ads, and analytics. Does that work?"

Why? Because interviewers sometimes introduce features mid-interview to test how you handle scope changes. If you've already named them as out of scope, you can say: "That's a great point — I'd treat that as a Phase 2 feature. Here's how I'd add it without redesigning the core..."

### Mermaid: Requirements Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Hear the problem] --> Ask1[Ask: What are the top 3 features?]
    Ask1 --> Ask2[Ask: What scale? DAU?]
    Ask2 --> Ask3[Ask: Latency and availability SLA?]
    Ask3 --> Ask4[Ask: Consistency vs availability?]
    Ask4 --> Summarise[Summarise back and confirm]
    Summarise --> InScope{Confirmed scope?}
    InScope -->|No| Ask1
    InScope -->|Yes| NextPhase[Proceed to API Design]
```

### Requirements Summary Template

After the questions, say:

> "Let me summarise what I'll build. Functionally: [list 3 core features]. Non-functionally: [scale], [latency target], [availability level], [consistency model]. Out of scope: [list excluded features]. Does this capture what you have in mind?"

### Interview Dialogue: Full Requirements Phase for Dropbox

> You: "What are the core features? Should I focus on file upload, download, and sync, or also collaboration and sharing?"
> Interviewer: "Focus on upload, download, and sync."
> You: "Great. How many users — tens of millions or hundreds of millions?"
> Interviewer: "Let's say 50 million DAU."
> You: "What's the average file size people upload?"
> Interviewer: "Mix of documents and photos — average maybe 5 MB."
> You: "Okay. Latency target — is this primarily about throughput (can I take 30 seconds to sync a 100 MB file) or do users expect near-real-time sync?"
> Interviewer: "Near-real-time for small edits, throughput matters more for large uploads."
> You: "Availability — 99.9% or higher? And can we tolerate losing an uploaded file?"
> Interviewer: "High availability, no data loss ever."
> You: "Perfect. So: 50M DAU, upload/download/sync of avg 5 MB files, near-real-time sync for small edits, 99.9%+ availability, zero data loss. Out of scope: collaboration, versioning beyond basic, sharing. Let me start on the API."

### Common Mistakes

1. **Asking only about features, not about scale**. Scale drives architecture choices more than features do.
2. **Accepting vague answers**. Push for numbers — order of magnitude is enough.
3. **Spending more than 5 minutes here**. Requirements is a conversation, not an interrogation. Get the essentials and move on.
keyTakeaways: Functional = what it does (features). Non-functional = how well it does it (scale, latency, availability).
Always extract a DAU number — ask for an order of magnitude if needed
Ask about consistency vs availability for every distributed system
Name what is out of scope explicitly — it protects you when scope changes mid-interview

sessions.title: API Design Best Practices
sessions.paretoJustification: API design shows product thinking and technical rigour simultaneously. A well-designed API signals senior-level judgment.
objectives: Design RESTful APIs with correct resource naming, HTTP verbs, and status codes
Define request and response schemas for core endpoints
Identify when to use REST vs gRPC vs GraphQL

activities.description: Design the full API for a URL shortener: create, redirect, delete, stats
Design the API for a messaging app (send, receive, mark-read, history)
Compare REST vs gRPC trade-offs for the messaging API

reviewQuestions: What is the correct HTTP verb for creating a short URL? For updating it? For redirecting to the original?
Why should you use pagination on list endpoints by default?
When would you choose gRPC over REST?
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a complete REST API for any core system with correct verbs, resource paths, and schemas in under 5 minutes.
sessions.content:
## API Design Best Practices

The API phase is often treated as a box-checking exercise. Candidates write a few endpoints and move on. But interviewers watch carefully for product thinking ("did they consider the consumer?"), technical rigour ("did they use the right HTTP verbs?"), and future-proofing ("did they version the API?").

### REST Fundamentals

REST (Representational State Transfer) is the default for public APIs in interview context unless the interviewer specifies otherwise.

**Resource naming**: use nouns, not verbs. The URL identifies the resource; the HTTP verb identifies the action.

```
Good:  GET  /users/{id}/tweets
Bad:   GET  /getUserTweets?userId=123

Good:  POST /urls
Bad:   POST /createUrl
```

**HTTP verbs**:

| Verb | Use | Idempotent? |
|------|-----|------------|
| GET | Read a resource | Yes |
| POST | Create a new resource | No |
| PUT | Replace a resource entirely | Yes |
| PATCH | Partially update a resource | No (can be made yes) |
| DELETE | Remove a resource | Yes |

**Status codes** (know these):
- 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content
- 301 Moved Permanently (for URL redirects), 302 Found (temporary redirect)
- 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests
- 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable

### Worked Example: URL Shortener API

```
# Create a short URL
POST /v1/urls
Request:  { "long_url": "https://example.com/very/long/path", "custom_alias": "mylink" }
Response: 201 { "short_url": "https://sho.rt/mylink", "url_id": "abc123", "expires_at": "2027-01-01" }

# Redirect (core read path)
GET /v1/{short_code}
Response: 301 Location: https://example.com/very/long/path

# Get URL stats
GET /v1/urls/{url_id}/stats
Response: 200 { "clicks": 42378, "unique_visitors": 18234, "top_countries": [...] }

# Delete a URL
DELETE /v1/urls/{url_id}
Response: 204 No Content
```

Note: version the API with `/v1/`. This lets you introduce `/v2/` without breaking existing clients.

### Worked Example: Twitter Timeline API

```
# Post a tweet
POST /v1/tweets
Request:  { "content": "Hello world", "media_ids": ["img123"] }
Response: 201 { "tweet_id": "t789", "created_at": "2026-03-22T10:00:00Z" }

# Read home timeline (paginated)
GET /v1/users/{user_id}/timeline?cursor=eyJpZCI6IjEyM...
Response: 200 {
  "tweets": [...],
  "next_cursor": "eyJpZCI6IjQ1...",
  "has_more": true
}

# Follow a user
POST /v1/users/{user_id}/followers
Request:  { "follower_id": "u456" }
Response: 201 { "followed_at": "2026-03-22T10:00:00Z" }

# Search tweets
GET /v1/search/tweets?q=systemdesign&limit=20&cursor=...
Response: 200 { "tweets": [...], "next_cursor": "..." }
```

### Pagination Patterns

Always paginate list endpoints. There are three approaches:

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[List endpoint] --> P{Pagination type?}
    P --> Off[Offset: ?page=3&limit=20\nSimple but slow at high offsets]
    P --> Cur[Cursor: ?cursor=abc\nFast, stable, handles inserts]
    P --> Key[Keyset: ?after_id=123\nFast, good for sorted lists]
    Off --> C1[Use for admin UIs with low record counts]
    Cur --> C2[Use for infinite scroll / feeds]
    Key --> C3[Use for time-ordered results]
```

For social feeds, always use cursor-based pagination. Offset pagination is O(n) at the database level.

### REST vs gRPC vs GraphQL

| Protocol | When to use |
|----------|------------|
| REST | Public APIs, browser clients, simple CRUD |
| gRPC | Internal microservices, high-throughput, strongly typed contracts |
| GraphQL | When clients need flexible queries and you want to avoid over-fetching |

For internal service-to-service calls in a large system, mention gRPC: "For the notification service to communication service calls, I'd use gRPC for lower latency and strong typing."

### API Security Checklist

Mention at least one of these:
- **Authentication**: JWT bearer tokens or API keys in the Authorization header
- **Rate limiting**: 429 Too Many Requests; sliding window per user, per IP
- **Input validation**: reject malformed requests early (400 Bad Request)
- **Idempotency keys**: for write operations that should not be duplicated (e.g., payment processing)

### Common Mistakes

1. **Using verbs in URLs**. `/getUser` is wrong; `/users/{id}` is correct.
2. **Skipping pagination**. Any endpoint that returns a list must be paginated.
3. **Forgetting API versioning**. Always prefix with `/v1/`.
4. **Not defining the response schema**. Show what fields are returned, not just the status code.
keyTakeaways: REST: nouns in URLs, verbs via HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE)
Always paginate list endpoints — use cursor pagination for feeds
Version your API from day one: /v1/
For internal service calls, gRPC is faster and type-safe

sessions.title: Database Selection Decision Tree
sessions.paretoJustification: Database choice is the highest-impact single decision in a system design. Getting it wrong means the whole design fails to scale.
objectives: Apply the database selection decision tree to any given access pattern
Justify a database choice with at least 2 specific technical reasons
Know the scaling limits of each database type

activities.description: Study the decision tree and memorise the 7 database types with their primary use cases
Practice database selection for 6 systems: Twitter, Uber, YouTube, WhatsApp, Google Maps, LinkedIn
For each choice, write a 2-sentence justification and one alternative with its trade-off

reviewQuestions: A system needs to store 100 billion social graph edges and supports 'find mutual friends' queries. What database do you use?
A real-time leaderboard needs to update and retrieve top-100 scores under 10ms. What technology?
When does eventual consistency make a relational database inappropriate?
sessions.successCriteria: Can select the correct database type for any system and justify it with two specific technical reasons in under 60 seconds.
sessions.content:
## Database Selection Decision Tree

The database selection question is a trap for candidates who default to PostgreSQL for everything. A senior engineer selects the database that best fits the **access pattern, scale, and consistency requirements** — not the most familiar one.

### The Full Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[What is the primary access pattern?] --> A{ACID transactions\nand complex joins?}
    A -->|Yes| SQL[Relational: PostgreSQL / MySQL\nBest for: user accounts, orders, payments]
    A -->|No| B{Flexible schema,\nnested documents?}
    B -->|Yes| Doc[Document: MongoDB / Firestore\nBest for: product catalogs, user profiles]
    B -->|No| C{High-cardinality\ntime-series data?}
    C -->|Yes| TS[Time-series: InfluxDB / TimescaleDB\nBest for: metrics, IoT, monitoring]
    C -->|No| D{Full-text search\nor faceting?}
    D -->|Yes| ES[Search: Elasticsearch / OpenSearch\nBest for: product search, log analysis]
    D -->|No| E{Graph traversals:\nfriends-of-friends,\nshortest path?}
    E -->|Yes| Graph[Graph: Neo4j / Amazon Neptune\nBest for: social graphs, fraud detection]
    E -->|No| F{Massive write\nthroughput, known\naccess patterns?}
    F -->|Yes| WC[Wide-column: Cassandra / DynamoDB\nBest for: time-series events, messaging]
    F -->|No| Cache[In-memory: Redis\nBest for: cache, sessions, leaderboards]
```

### Database Quick-Reference

| Database | Type | Sweet Spot | Scaling Limit |
|----------|------|-----------|---------------|
| PostgreSQL | Relational | ACID, complex queries | ~10B rows per table, shard beyond |
| MySQL | Relational | Read-heavy, web apps | Similar to PostgreSQL |
| MongoDB | Document | Flexible schema, nested | Horizontal sharding built-in |
| Cassandra | Wide-column | Write-heavy, time-series | Virtually unlimited with partitioning |
| DynamoDB | Wide-column | Serverless, variable load | Unlimited (managed) |
| Redis | In-memory | Cache, pub-sub, leaderboards | RAM-bound, ~hundreds of GB per node |
| Elasticsearch | Search | Full-text, aggregations | Horizontal sharding |
| Neo4j | Graph | Graph traversals | ~100B nodes (enterprise) |
| InfluxDB | Time-series | Metrics at high frequency | Built-in time-based partitioning |

### Decision Examples

**Twitter — tweets storage**:
- Access pattern: write once, read many times by user_id and time
- Wide-column wins: Cassandra with `(user_id, tweet_id)` partition key
- NOT PostgreSQL: at 1.1 trillion rows over 10 years, a single relational DB requires extreme sharding

**WhatsApp — messages**:
- Access pattern: write once, read sequentially by conversation
- Wide-column wins: Cassandra with `(conversation_id, message_timestamp)` clustering key
- Delivers natural time ordering within a partition

**Uber — trip history**:
- Access pattern: write once (end of trip), read occasionally by user or driver
- PostgreSQL works: ~20M trips/day × 3,650 days = 73B rows — borderline, may need sharding
- Alternative: Cassandra for write volume, with PostgreSQL for analytics (CQRS pattern)

**YouTube — video metadata**:
- Access pattern: read-heavy (1B views/day), relatively few writes
- Wide-column (Bigtable): Google uses this internally; supports massive read throughput
- For interviews: Cassandra or DynamoDB are acceptable substitutes

**LinkedIn — people graph**:
- Access pattern: "find 2nd degree connections", "mutual connections"
- Graph database wins: Neo4j handles multi-hop traversals natively; SQL requires expensive self-joins

### The Polyglot Persistence Pattern

Real production systems use multiple databases for different purposes:

```
PostgreSQL  → User accounts, payments (ACID required)
Cassandra   → Social feed events (write-heavy)
Redis       → Session cache, rate limiting, leaderboards
Elasticsearch → User and content search
S3          → Raw media files (not a database, but object storage)
```

In an interview, mentioning polyglot persistence signals senior-level awareness. Caveat: acknowledge the operational complexity it introduces.

### Justification Template

When you state your choice, always give two reasons:

> "I'd use Cassandra for the tweet store because: first, our write QPS is 3,500 with no joins required, which matches Cassandra's write-optimised log-structured storage. Second, our access pattern is always by user_id and time range, which maps cleanly to a partition key and clustering key — avoiding scatter-gather queries."

### Common Mistakes

1. **Defaulting to PostgreSQL for everything**. It's a great database but it's not a wide-column store, graph database, or search engine.
2. **Choosing MongoDB "for flexibility"**. This is not a justification. MongoDB trades consistency and join support for flexible schema — name the actual trade-off.
3. **Not mentioning polyglot persistence**. Large systems almost always use multiple databases.
keyTakeaways: Choose database by access pattern first, then scale second
Cassandra/DynamoDB: massive write throughput with known access patterns
Redis: cache and real-time features (leaderboards, sessions, pub-sub)
Graph DB: only when you need multi-hop graph traversals — overkill otherwise

sessions.title: Drawing Architecture Diagrams
sessions.paretoJustification: The diagram is what the interviewer photographs and scores. A clean, correctly-layered diagram communicates more than 10 minutes of verbal explanation.
objectives: Draw a standard 5-layer architecture diagram (client, CDN, LB, services, storage)
Use consistent notation for components (boxes, arrows, labels)
Walk through the diagram narrating a read path and a write path

activities.description: Practice drawing the 5-layer template diagram from memory in 5 minutes
Draw the architecture for a URL shortener: full diagram with all components labelled
Narrate the read path and write path for the URL shortener diagram out loud

reviewQuestions: What 5 layers should every web architecture diagram include?
How do you show a cache layer in relation to the database?
What is a message queue's role in an architecture diagram and when should you add one?
sessions.successCriteria: Can draw a complete, correctly-layered architecture diagram for any standard system in under 10 minutes.
sessions.content:
## Drawing Architecture Diagrams

The architecture diagram is the centrepiece of the interview. Interviewers spend more time looking at your diagram than listening to your words. A well-drawn diagram communicates structure, data flow, and component relationships instantly. A messy diagram undermines confidence even if your verbal explanation is correct.

### The 5-Layer Template

Every web architecture has the same skeleton. Memorise this template:

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Clients
        Web[Web Browser]
        Mobile[Mobile App]
    end
    subgraph Edge
        CDN[CDN\nStatic assets, video]
        LB[Load Balancer\nL7, sticky sessions]
    end
    subgraph Application
        S1[Service A\nAPI Gateway]
        S2[Service B\nBusiness Logic]
    end
    subgraph Storage
        Cache[Redis Cache]
        DB[(Primary DB)]
        DBR[(Read Replica)]
        Queue[Message Queue]
    end
    subgraph Async
        Worker[Worker/Consumer]
        ObjStore[Object Storage]
    end
    Web --> CDN
    Web --> LB
    Mobile --> LB
    LB --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> Cache
    S2 --> DB
    S2 --> DBR
    S2 --> Queue
    Queue --> Worker
    Worker --> ObjStore
```

### Notation Rules

Use these conventions consistently:

| Component | Shape | Label |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| User/client | Rectangle | "Web Browser", "Mobile App" |
| Load balancer | Rectangle with shadow or diamond | "LB" or "Load Balancer" |
| Service/server | Rectangle | Service name + responsibility |
| Database | Cylinder | DB type + name |
| Cache | Rectangle with dashed border | "Redis" + data it holds |
| Queue | Parallelogram or labelled rectangle | "Kafka", "SQS" |
| CDN | Cloud shape | "CloudFront", "CDN" |
| Object storage | Rectangle | "S3" |
| Arrow direction | → | Data/request flow direction |
| Bidirectional | ↔ | Only when truly bidirectional |

### Labelling Best Practices

- Label every arrow: what flows through it ("HTTP GET /tweets", "gRPC read request", "kafka message")
- Label every component with its technology: "Redis (cache)" not just "cache"
- Add approximate numbers where known: "100K QPS" on the load balancer arrow
- Highlight the component you're currently discussing (circle it, asterisk it)

### The Walk-Through Script

After drawing, narrate both paths:

**Read path** ("Let me walk through what happens when a user reads their timeline"):
> "The mobile app sends a GET /timeline request. It hits the load balancer which routes to one of our application servers. The app server first checks the Redis cache — if there's a cache hit, we return the timeline immediately in under 10ms. On a cache miss, we query the PostgreSQL read replica, format the response, populate the cache with a 60-second TTL, and return to the client."

**Write path** ("Now let me walk through what happens when a user posts a tweet"):
> "A POST /tweets request hits the load balancer, goes to the write service. The write service validates the request, writes to the primary PostgreSQL database, and publishes a TweetCreated event to Kafka. The fanout service consumes the event and writes the tweet ID to the Redis timeline caches of all followers. This is asynchronous — the POST returns a 201 to the client without waiting for fan-out to complete."

### What to Draw First

Do NOT start at the left and work right. Draw in this order:
1. Clients (top left)
2. Load balancer (after clients)
3. Core services (middle)
4. Primary database (right side)
5. Cache layer (between services and DB)
6. Message queue + workers (bottom, if async processing exists)
7. CDN + object storage (top, if media/static assets)
8. Read replicas (next to primary DB)

This order matches the data flow and looks intentional.

### Red Flags Interviewers Look For

- **No load balancer**: single point of failure, signals ignorance of HA
- **Services writing directly to read replicas**: replicas are read-only
- **No cache between service and DB at high QPS**: design can't scale
- **Synchronous fan-out**: at Twitter scale, synchronous writes to 100M follower caches is a deadlock
- **No CDN for media**: media at scale must be served from the edge

### Common Mistakes

1. **Drawing too small**. Leave space to add components as the conversation evolves.
2. **Not labelling arrows**. An unlabelled arrow is ambiguous — is it HTTP? gRPC? A database query?
3. **Drawing all components simultaneously**. Build the diagram incrementally, narrating as you draw.
4. **No redundancy shown**. Show primary + replica for databases, multiple instances for services.
keyTakeaways: Every architecture has 5 layers: clients, edge (CDN/LB), services, storage, async
Label every component with its technology and every arrow with what flows through it
Walk through both read path and write path explicitly after drawing
Draw incrementally while narrating — do not draw in silence

sessions.title: Scaling Discussion Framework
sessions.paretoJustification: Scaling is the explicit second half of every system design interview. Candidates who cannot scale their initial design fail even if the initial design is correct.
objectives: Identify the bottleneck in any given design from first principles
Apply the correct scaling solution for each bottleneck type
Structure the scaling discussion as a sequence of problem → solution → trade-off

activities.description: Study the 8-problem scaling toolkit and memorise one solution per problem
Given the Twitter architecture, identify and rank the top 3 bottlenecks
For each bottleneck, state the problem, your solution, and the trade-off it introduces

reviewQuestions: Your read QPS has grown 10× and the database is the bottleneck. Walk through your scaling plan.
What is the trade-off introduced by database sharding?
When does adding more servers stop helping and what do you do instead?
sessions.successCriteria: Can identify the top bottleneck in any design and articulate a solution with its trade-off in under 2 minutes.
sessions.content:
## Scaling Discussion Framework

Scaling is not about randomly adding caches and queues. It's about diagnosing a bottleneck and applying a targeted fix. Interviewers who see a candidate throw technologies at a problem without identifying the root cause will rate them poorly, even if the technologies are individually correct.

### The Scaling Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Identify bottleneck] --> B[Propose solution]
    B --> C[State trade-off]
    C --> D{New bottleneck\ncreated?}
    D -->|Yes| A
    D -->|No| E[Done for this path]
```

Scaling is iterative. Every solution introduces a new constraint. Show the interviewer you understand this cycle.

### Bottleneck Identification

Ask: "What breaks first when traffic 10×s?"

The answer is almost always one of:
1. **Read QPS exceeds database capacity** → add cache and/or read replicas
2. **Write QPS exceeds database capacity** → shard the database or switch to Cassandra
3. **Storage capacity exceeded** → add nodes / move to distributed storage
4. **Single server is a SPOF** → add redundancy, multi-AZ
5. **Network egress is too high** → CDN for cacheable content
6. **Services are tightly coupled** → decouple with message queue
7. **All traffic hits one service** → load balance + stateless services
8. **Query latency is high** → add index, denormalise, or cache query results

### The Scaling Toolkit

| Problem | Primary Solution | Trade-off |
|---------|----------------|----------|
| Read QPS overload | Read replicas + Redis cache | Replication lag; cache staleness |
| Write QPS overload | Sharding | Cross-shard queries become expensive |
| Large media storage | Object storage (S3) + CDN | Eventual consistency in CDN |
| Tight coupling | Kafka message queue | Added latency; at-least-once delivery |
| Global latency | Multi-region + CDN PoPs | Data replication complexity |
| SPOF | Redundancy + health checks | Higher cost; failover complexity |
| Slow queries | Indexing / denormalisation | Write overhead; storage increase |
| Thundering herd | Cache warming + jitter | Warm-up time after deployments |

### Worked Example: Scaling Twitter

**Initial design**: single PostgreSQL DB, several app servers, no cache.

**Round 1 — Read bottleneck**:
> "At 100K read QPS, our single PostgreSQL instance will saturate at ~5,000 QPS. First fix: add Redis cache for timelines. With an 80% hit rate, we reduce DB reads to 20K QPS. We also add 5 read replicas to handle the remaining read traffic."

Trade-off: timeline cache may show tweets 60 seconds stale. Acceptable for social feeds.

**Round 2 — Write bottleneck**:
> "Write QPS is 10K — our single primary handles ~5K comfortably, but at peak it's saturated. We add a message queue (Kafka) between the write service and the database. Kafka absorbs write bursts and the consumers write to the DB at a controlled rate."

Trade-off: writes are no longer immediately consistent — a tweet may take 1-2 seconds to appear.

**Round 3 — Fan-out bottleneck**:
> "When a celebrity with 10M followers tweets, synchronous fan-out to all 10M timeline caches would take too long. We switch to hybrid fan-out: push to small accounts (< 10K followers), pull from celebrity feed on read for large accounts."

Trade-off: timeline reads for celebrity-follower-heavy users now involve a merge step.

**Round 4 — Media bandwidth**:
> "Media egress at ~10 Gbps. We add a CDN (CloudFront) in front of our media storage (S3). With a 95% CDN hit rate, origin servers only see 500 Mbps."

Trade-off: CDN cost; freshness delay for newly uploaded media.

### How to Present Scaling Discussion

Structure each scaling point as three sentences:
1. **Bottleneck**: "The database becomes the read bottleneck because..."
2. **Solution**: "I would address this by adding..."
3. **Trade-off**: "The trade-off is that... which is acceptable because..."

This structure signals clarity of thinking. Rambling about solutions without naming trade-offs is a yellow flag.

### Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

| Type | What it means | When to use |
|------|--------------|------------|
| Vertical | Bigger machine (more CPU/RAM) | Quick fix, limited ceiling, no code change |
| Horizontal | More machines | Unlimited scale, requires statelessness |

Always mention that stateless services (no in-memory session state) are a prerequisite for horizontal scaling.

### Common Mistakes

1. **"Just add more servers"**. This only works for stateless compute layers. It doesn't help database write throughput.
2. **Not naming trade-offs**. Every solution has a downside. Omitting it signals shallow understanding.
3. **Over-engineering the initial design**. Start simple, then scale. Kafka in the initial design before you've even estimated write QPS is premature.
keyTakeaways: Identify the bottleneck first; do not add infrastructure randomly
Every scaling solution introduces a trade-off — state it explicitly
The scaling loop: identify bottleneck → propose solution → state trade-off → find new bottleneck
Read overload: cache + read replicas. Write overload: sharding or Kafka. Media: CDN.

sessions.title: Handling Follow-up Questions
sessions.paretoJustification: Follow-up questions reveal how well you understand your own design. Answering them well separates senior candidates from junior ones.
objectives: Recognise the 5 categories of follow-up questions and their intent
Answer failure-scenario questions with a structured circuit-breaker / fallback response
Adapt the design when the interviewer changes a constraint mid-interview

activities.description: Study the 5 follow-up question categories and one response template per category
Practice answering 10 follow-up questions for a Twitter design under timed conditions
Practice adapting a URL shortener design when the interviewer says 'now scale to 10× the traffic'

reviewQuestions: Your database primary goes down during peak traffic. Walk me through what happens.
The interviewer changes the consistency requirement from eventual to strong mid-interview. How do you adapt?
What is a circuit breaker and when would you use one?
sessions.successCriteria: Can confidently answer any of the 5 follow-up question categories without losing composure or going blank.
sessions.content:
## Handling Follow-up Questions

Follow-up questions are not trick questions. They are an opportunity for the interviewer to probe the depth of your understanding. A candidate who designed a system well but cannot answer follow-ups will still receive a lower score than one who designed adequately and handled follow-ups with confidence.

### The 5 Follow-up Question Categories

```mermaid
graph TD
    FU[Follow-up Question] --> F1[Failure Scenarios\n"What if X goes down?"]  
    FU --> F2[Scale Changes\n"Now handle 10× traffic"]
    FU --> F3[Trade-off Challenges\n"Why not use Y instead?"] 
    FU --> F4[Edge Cases\n"What if two users do X simultaneously?"]
    FU --> F5[Operational Questions\n"How do you deploy this?"]
```

### Category 1: Failure Scenarios

**Typical questions**:
- "What if the database goes down?"
- "What if a Kafka broker fails?"
- "What if your cache is cold after a restart?"

**Response template**: State the failure mode, the detection mechanism, the recovery mechanism, and the user impact.

> "If the primary database fails: first, our monitoring (Prometheus + PagerDuty) detects the failure within 30 seconds via health checks. AWS RDS Multi-AZ automatically promotes a read replica to primary — this takes ~60 seconds. During that window, writes fail with a 503. We handle this with a retry queue in the client SDK: writes are buffered and retried when the primary is back. Reads continue normally from the replica."

**Pattern: Circuit Breaker**

When a downstream service fails repeatedly, stop sending it requests to let it recover:
```
Closed (normal) → [too many failures] → Open (reject all) → [timeout] → Half-Open (try one) → [success] → Closed
```

Mentioning circuit breakers signals production experience.

### Category 2: Scale Changes

**Typical questions**:
- "Now imagine traffic 10× overnight — what changes?"
- "What if we need to go global?"

**Response template**: run through the bottleneck identification loop from Session 6.

> "At 10× traffic — so 1M read QPS instead of 100K — the first bottleneck is the cache. Our current 2 Redis nodes handle 200K ops/s; at 1M we'd need 10 nodes. The database read replicas are fine at that QPS since most reads are cache hits. Write QPS would be 100K — beyond a single primary. I'd introduce write sharding by user_id. Cross-shard queries (e.g., trending topics) would use a separate analytics DB."

### Category 3: Trade-off Challenges

**Typical questions**:
- "Why Cassandra over DynamoDB?"
- "Why not use a relational database instead of Kafka?"
- "Why did you choose eventual consistency?"

**Response template**: acknowledge the alternative is valid, then give 2 reasons your choice fits better.

> "DynamoDB would also work well here — it's fully managed and scales automatically. I chose Cassandra because we have an on-premises team familiar with it, and we need fine-grained control over compaction strategies for our write-heavy workload. If we were starting greenfield on AWS, DynamoDB would be my first choice."

Showing you understand multiple valid approaches signals seniority.

### Category 4: Edge Cases

**Typical questions**:
- "What if two users try to claim the same username simultaneously?"
- "What if the same user submits the same request twice?"

**Response template**: idempotency and distributed locking.

> "For duplicate username claims, we use a unique index on the username column in PostgreSQL. Two concurrent INSERTs will race, and only one will succeed — the other gets a unique constraint violation and we return a 409 Conflict to that user. For client-side retries, we generate an idempotency key on the client, and the server deduplicates based on that key stored in Redis with a 24-hour TTL."

### Category 5: Operational Questions

**Typical questions**:
- "How do you deploy without downtime?"
- "How do you monitor this?"
- "How do you handle schema migrations?"

**Response templates**:
- **Deployment**: "Blue-green deployment or rolling deployment with health checks. Traffic shifts gradually using the load balancer's weighted routing."
- **Monitoring**: "Prometheus + Grafana for metrics. P99 latency, error rate, and QPS are the key SLOs. PagerDuty for alerting."
- **Schema migrations**: "Online schema changes using pt-online-schema-change or gh-ost for large tables. Never block the table lock on a live system."

### When You Don't Know the Answer

Do not bluff. Interviewers respect intellectual honesty:

> "I don't know the exact implementation of how [database X] handles that internally. My instinct is [hypothesis]. I'd validate that by checking the official docs and running a benchmark before committing to that choice in production."

This is better than confidently stating something incorrect.

### Common Mistakes

1. **Going blank on failure questions**. Every component can fail. Pre-think the failure mode for each component in your design.
2. **Rejecting the interviewer's alternative**. Always acknowledge it has merit before explaining why your choice fits better.
3. **Not adapting when constraints change**. The interviewer changes constraints to see if your design is rigid or flexible.
keyTakeaways: 5 follow-up categories: failure, scale change, trade-off challenge, edge case, operational
Failure template: detection → recovery mechanism → user impact during window
Trade-off template: acknowledge the alternative, give 2 reasons your choice fits better
When you don't know: state your hypothesis and how you'd validate it — never bluff

sessions.title: Common Interviewer Probes and How to Answer
sessions.paretoJustification: There are ~20 questions that appear in 80% of system design interviews. Learning the pattern for each eliminates surprise and panic.
objectives: Recognise the 10 most common system design interview probes
Give a structured 2–3 sentence answer for each probe
Adapt the answer when the interviewer follows up

activities.description: Study all 10 probes and their response patterns
Practice rapid-fire: partner reads a probe, you answer in under 60 seconds
For each answer, practice the follow-up: interviewer challenges your answer, you defend or pivot

reviewQuestions: How would you answer: 'How do you ensure data consistency across microservices?'
How would you answer: 'How do you prevent hotspot problems in your sharding scheme?'
What is the 2-sentence answer to: 'How does your system handle a 10× spike in traffic?'
sessions.successCriteria: Can answer all 10 common probes clearly and confidently in under 60 seconds each.
sessions.content:
## Common Interviewer Probes and How to Answer

Experienced interviewers have a playbook. They ask variations of the same ~20 questions across every candidate. Once you know the playbook, you can prepare a structured response for each. This is not about memorising answers — it's about having a framework so you can respond clearly under pressure.

### The 10 Most Common Probes

**Probe 1: "How would you handle database failures?"**

Structure: detection → automatic failover → user impact → data safety

> "We detect failure via health checks every 30 seconds. If 3 consecutive checks fail, the monitoring system triggers an alert and, for managed databases (RDS Multi-AZ), automatic promotion of the replica. During the ~60-second failover window, writes queue in a retry buffer at the client. Reads continue from the replica. No data loss because of synchronous replication to the standby."

**Probe 2: "How do you scale the database?"**

Structure: identify whether it's read or write bound → apply correct solution

> "First I'd determine if it's read-bound or write-bound. If read-bound, I add read replicas and a cache layer. If write-bound, I shard by the primary access key — for example, user_id — so writes are distributed. I'd also consider switching to Cassandra for write-heavy workloads with known access patterns."

**Probe 3: "How do you ensure consistency across microservices?"**

Structure: name the pattern → explain the guarantee

> "I'd use the Saga pattern with choreography. Each service publishes a domain event on success; downstream services react to that event. If a step fails, a compensating transaction reverses upstream operations. For strict consistency, I'd use two-phase commit or a distributed transaction coordinator, but only when the use case truly requires it — the overhead is significant."

**Probe 4: "How do you prevent hot partitions in your sharding scheme?"**

Structure: identify why hotspots occur → solution

> "Hotspots occur when many requests map to the same shard — for example, sharding by user_id where celebrity users generate 1000× more traffic. Solutions: use consistent hashing to distribute load more evenly; add a virtual node layer so a hot user can be split across multiple shards; or apply application-level rate limiting per user so no single shard is overwhelmed."

**Probe 5: "How does your system handle a 10× traffic spike?"**

Structure: auto-scaling → rate limiting → graceful degradation

> "At the compute tier, auto-scaling groups add instances within 2–3 minutes based on CPU and QPS metrics. For the database, the cache absorbs most of the read amplification. For write spikes, the message queue (Kafka) acts as a buffer — the database processes at its sustainable rate. If traffic exceeds even that, we apply rate limiting (429 Too Many Requests) and queue overflow to a dead-letter queue for later processing."

**Probe 6: "What happens if your cache goes down?"**

Structure: impact → fallback → recovery

> "Cache failure causes all reads to hit the database directly — a 'thundering herd'. We handle this with two mechanisms: first, our cache is deployed as a Redis Sentinel cluster with automatic failover, so individual node failure is handled transparently. Second, if the entire cache cluster fails, circuit breakers on the service layer detect the error rate spike and apply rate limiting so the database isn't overwhelmed. We'd also warm the new cache from the database before removing the rate limit."

**Probe 7: "How do you handle duplicate requests?"**

Structure: idempotency key → server-side deduplication

> "Clients include an idempotency key (UUID) in the request header for any mutating operation. The server stores processed idempotency keys in Redis with a 24-hour TTL. On receiving a request, the server checks Redis first — if the key exists, it returns the cached response without reprocessing. This guarantees exactly-once semantics from the user's perspective."

**Probe 8: "How do you deploy zero-downtime updates?"**

Structure: strategy → rollback plan

> "We use rolling deployments with blue-green switch-over. New instances are spun up with the new version; the load balancer gradually shifts traffic (canary: 1% → 10% → 100%) while monitoring error rates and p99 latency. If either metric degrades, we roll back by shifting traffic back to the old instances. Database migrations use pt-online-schema-change to avoid locking the table."

**Probe 9: "How do you monitor this system?"**

Structure: metrics → alerting → dashboards

> "Three golden signals: latency (p50, p99 per endpoint), error rate (4xx and 5xx), and QPS. Prometheus scrapes all services every 15 seconds. Grafana dashboards show per-service SLOs. PagerDuty triggers on-call when p99 > SLA threshold for 2 consecutive minutes. For business metrics, we emit events to Kafka and consume them into a data warehouse for analytics."

**Probe 10: "What are the main trade-offs in your design?"**

Structure: name the trade-off → explain what you gave up and why it's acceptable

> "Three main trade-offs: first, eventual consistency on the timeline — users may see tweets up to 60 seconds late, which is acceptable for a social feed but not for financial data. Second, fan-out on write increases write amplification for celebrity users — we mitigate this with hybrid fan-out. Third, Kafka adds operational complexity and latency — writes take an extra 100–200ms, but the decoupling benefit (services can scale independently, retries are automatic) outweighs the cost."

### How to Improve Probe Fluency

Do the following drills:
1. Write all 10 probes on separate cards
2. Shuffle and pick a random card
3. Answer out loud in under 60 seconds
4. Repeat daily for 2 weeks

Fluency comes from repetition, not from reading the answers once.

### Common Mistakes

1. **Giving one-word answers**: "We'd use Redis." — This is not an answer; it's a vocabulary test. Always explain *why*.
2. **Speculating without signalling uncertainty**: "I think Cassandra handles this by..." — Say "my understanding is" or "I'd verify this, but..."
3. **Getting defensive when challenged**: The interviewer challenging you is often a test of intellectual flexibility, not a signal that you are wrong.
keyTakeaways: There are ~10 probes that appear in 80% of interviews — drill them to fluency
Every answer needs: what you do + why + what you give up
Idempotency key + Redis dedup = the standard answer for duplicate request handling
Rolling canary deployment + p99 monitoring = the standard answer for zero-downtime deploys

sessions.title: Time Management in a 45-Minute Interview
sessions.paretoJustification: Running out of time before reaching the architecture phase is the single most common reason candidates who know the material still fail.
objectives: Internalise the 5/5/5/20/10 minute RADIO time budget
Identify the time-sink traps that cause overruns in each phase
Practice cutting a phase short gracefully when time is running low

activities.description: Do a timed run-through of requirements-only phase for 3 different problems, targeting exactly 5 minutes each
Practise a full 45-minute timed mock interview and identify which phase overran
Practice the 'time warning' pivot: 'I notice we're at the 30-minute mark, let me move to the scaling discussion'

reviewQuestions: You're 15 minutes into requirements and haven't drawn anything. What do you do?
How do you tell the interviewer you're moving to the next phase without being rude?
Which phase do most candidates cut short and what does that cost them?
sessions.successCriteria: Can complete a full 45-minute mock interview with all 5 RADIO phases covered in approximately the right time.
sessions.content:
## Time Management in a 45-Minute Interview

The most technically brilliant candidates can fail a system design interview simply by running out of time. They spend 25 minutes on requirements and API design, draw the architecture in 5 minutes, and never reach the scaling discussion — which is often where the highest-weighted scoring happens.

### The Time Budget

```
00:00 – 05:00   Requirements clarification (R)
05:00 – 10:00   API design + data model (A + D)
10:00 – 30:00   Core architecture — draw + narrate (I)
30:00 – 40:00   Scaling discussion (O)
40:00 – 45:00   Wrap-up, trade-off summary, Q&A
```

Print this. Tape it to your monitor. Internalise it.

### Why Candidates Overrun Requirements

Requirements discussions feel productive. The candidate is asking smart questions, the interviewer is engaged, and conversation flows naturally. But at minute 10, you've used twice your budget and haven't drawn a single component.

**Trap 1**: Asking too many questions. Stick to the playbook (features, scale, latency, consistency). Do not explore every edge case.

**Trap 2**: Over-documenting. Write 5 bullet points, not a specification document.

**Trap 3**: Waiting for perfect information. Interviewers will sometimes withhold information to see if you can proceed with uncertainty. Make an assumption, state it, and move on.

### Phase Transitions

Always announce phase transitions:

> "I think I have enough to start the design. Let me sketch out the API first, and then I'll jump into the architecture diagram. Feel free to stop me if I'm missing anything."

This keeps the interviewer oriented and signals structure.

### The Time Warning

Watching the clock is your responsibility. If you notice you're approaching a phase boundary, say so:

> "I notice we're at the 20-minute mark. I want to make sure I have time to cover the scaling discussion, so I'm going to move on from the initial architecture — does that work?"

This shows meta-awareness and professionalism. Interviewers appreciate it.

### What to Cut When Time is Short

If you're running behind, cut in this order (lowest to highest value):
1. **Detailed API schemas** — mention them verbally instead of writing them out
2. **Secondary use cases** — focus on the happy path
3. **Operational details** — deployment and monitoring can be mentioned briefly

NEVER cut:
- The core architecture diagram (this is scored most heavily)
- At least one scaling point (a design with no scaling discussion looks incomplete)

### The 45-Minute Map

```mermaid
gantt
    title 45-Minute Interview Timeline
    dateFormat  mm
    axisFormat  %M min
    section R Requirements
    Clarify features, scale, SLAs :r, 00, 5m
    section A + D
    API design + DB selection :ad, 05, 5m
    section I Infrastructure
    Draw architecture + walk through :i, 10, 20m
    section O Optimization
    Bottlenecks + scaling solutions :o, 30, 10m
    section Wrap-up
    Trade-offs + Q&A :w, 40, 5m
```

### Interview Dialogue: Managing Time

> You (at minute 5): "Great, I have enough context to start the design. Let me outline the API quickly and then I'll draw the architecture — I want to make sure we have time for the scaling discussion."
>
> Interviewer (at minute 20): "This is interesting — can you tell me more about how you'd handle the fan-out problem?"
>
> You: "Happy to — I'll spend 2 minutes on that and then move to the scaling discussion so we stay on track. The fan-out problem here is..."

You control the pacing. Politely but firmly.

### Building Time Awareness

During practice:
1. Set a visible timer for each phase
2. When the timer goes off, stop that phase immediately
3. Note what you didn't cover
4. In subsequent sessions, cut that part earlier to make room

After 5 practice sessions, phase transitions become automatic.

### Common Mistakes

1. **No clock awareness**. Many candidates never look at the time until the interviewer says "we have 5 minutes left".
2. **Letting the interviewer's questions derail the timeline**. Engage with questions briefly, then steer back to the plan.
3. **Skipping the wrap-up**. The final 5 minutes is when you score points on trade-offs and self-awareness. Do not let it disappear.
keyTakeaways: RADIO time budget: 5/5/5/20/10 minutes — the architecture phase gets 20 of the 45 minutes
Announce every phase transition: 'Let me move on to the API design now'
Watch the clock yourself — it is your responsibility, not the interviewer's
If running short, cut API detail first; never cut the architecture diagram or scaling discussion

sessions.title: Mock Interview Walkthrough
sessions.paretoJustification: Performance in a real interview requires practice under realistic conditions. Reading about the framework is necessary but not sufficient.
objectives: Complete a full 45-minute mock interview using the RADIO framework
Receive structured feedback against the 5 RADIO dimensions
Identify the 1–2 weakest areas and create a targeted improvement plan

activities.description: Full 45-minute mock interview: Design a ride-sharing service (Uber) — interviewer uses the probe playbook
Self-review against the 5-dimension scorecard: Requirements/API/Data/Infrastructure/Optimization
Write a 3-point improvement plan targeting the 2 lowest-scoring dimensions

reviewQuestions: After the mock, which of the 5 RADIO phases did you feel least confident in?
Did you mention trade-offs unprompted or only when the interviewer asked?
Did you finish all 5 phases within 45 minutes?
sessions.successCriteria: Can complete a full 45-minute mock interview with all 5 phases covered, proactive trade-off discussion, and no blank pauses longer than 30 seconds.
sessions.content:
## Mock Interview Walkthrough: Design Uber

This session is a scripted mock interview. Read both sides of the dialogue to understand what a passing performance looks like, then do the real thing with a partner.

### The Problem

> Interviewer: "Design Uber — the ride-sharing platform. You have 45 minutes."

### Phase R — Requirements (0:00–5:00)

> You: "Before I start designing, I'd like to clarify a few things. What are the core features in scope? I'm assuming rider matching with drivers, trip pricing, and real-time location tracking — does that sound right?"
>
> Interviewer: "Yes, focus on those three."
>
> You: "What scale should I target? How many daily active users?"
>
> Interviewer: "Let's say 100 million DAU, with 5 million active drivers."
>
> You: "Got it. Latency requirement — how quickly should a rider be matched with a driver?"
>
> Interviewer: "Under 10 seconds for a match. Real-time location updates should feel instant."
>
> You: "Availability — should this be 99.9% or higher? Can we tolerate a ride request failing occasionally?"
>
> Interviewer: "99.99%. We cannot drop ride requests."
>
> You: "Perfect. Out of scope: payments, ratings, surge pricing algorithm, driver onboarding. Is that right?"
>
> Interviewer: "Yes, that's fine."
>
> You: "So: 100M DAU, 5M drivers, real-time location, matching under 10 seconds, 99.99% availability. Let me start on the API."

### Phase A — API Design (5:00–8:00)

> You: "Three core APIs: one for drivers sending location updates, one for riders requesting a ride, and one for the matching result. Let me write them out."

```
POST /v1/drivers/{driver_id}/location
Request:  { "lat": 37.77, "lng": -122.41, "timestamp": "...", "status": "available" }
Response: 200 OK

POST /v1/rides/request
Request:  { "rider_id": "r123", "pickup_lat": 37.77, "pickup_lng": -122.41, "dropoff_lat": 37.78, "dropoff_lng": -122.40 }
Response: 202 { "ride_id": "ride789", "status": "searching" }

GET /v1/rides/{ride_id}/status
Response: 200 { "status": "matched", "driver": { "name": "...", "eta_seconds": 180, "vehicle": "..." } }
```

### Phase D — Data Model (8:00–11:00)

> You: "Two main data stores: Redis for real-time driver locations (in-memory, ephemeral), and PostgreSQL for ride history and user accounts (durable). I'll also use Kafka as a buffer for location updates."

```
Redis (geospatial index):
Key: drivers:available (Redis GEO set)
Value: { driver_id, lat, lng, last_seen }

PostgreSQL:
rides: ride_id, rider_id, driver_id, pickup_loc, dropoff_loc, status, fare, created_at
users: user_id, name, phone, rating, created_at
drivers: driver_id, name, vehicle, license, rating, created_at
```

### Phase I — Infrastructure (11:00–30:00)

> You: "Let me draw the architecture. I'll start with the driver location pipeline, since that's the highest volume flow — 5M drivers at 1 GPS update per 4 seconds is 1.25M writes per second."

*[Draws: Driver App → Kafka → Location Consumer → Redis GEO set]*

> You: "For ride matching: rider app sends a request to the matching service. The matching service queries Redis GEO with the rider's location — Redis GEORADIUS command returns nearby available drivers within 5 km. The matching service selects the nearest driver, updates both rider and driver status, and sends a push notification to the driver."

*[Draws: Rider App → LB → Matching Service → Redis, then Matching Service → Notification Service → Driver App]*

> You: "For trip history — once a trip completes, the trip service writes to PostgreSQL. This is low volume (at most 20M trips/day) so a single PostgreSQL primary with 2 read replicas is sufficient."

*[Draws: Trip Service → PostgreSQL Primary → 2 Read Replicas]*

### Phase O — Optimization (30:00–40:00)

> You: "Three main bottlenecks. First: location write volume. At 1.25M writes/sec, we need Kafka as a write buffer with ~13 Redis nodes for the write throughput. I've already incorporated that."
>
> "Second: matching service is stateless and can scale horizontally — just add instances behind the load balancer. The bottleneck is Redis GEO query performance. Redis GEORADIUS on a set with 5M entries runs in O(N+log N) where N is the result count — at our scale with appropriate search radius this is fast enough."
>
> "Third: availability at 99.99% means ≤52 minutes of downtime per year. Every component needs redundancy: Redis Sentinel for cache HA, Kafka with 3 replicas and rack-aware placement, PostgreSQL Multi-AZ with automatic failover, and the matching service behind a health-checked load balancer across 3 availability zones."

### Phase Wrap-Up (40:00–45:00)

> You: "Let me summarise the key trade-offs. First: I chose eventual consistency for location updates — if a driver's position is 4 seconds stale, that's acceptable for matching. If we needed sub-second freshness we'd need WebSockets instead of polling, adding complexity."
>
> "Second: I used Redis GEO instead of PostGIS — Redis is faster for real-time point-in-radius queries but loses durability. Driver locations are ephemeral so that's fine."
>
> "Third: the Kafka buffer adds ~100ms latency to location processing but gives us durability if the Redis cluster temporarily goes down — location updates are replayed on restart."
>
> Interviewer: "What happens if the matching service goes down mid-search?"
>
> You: "The rider receives a timeout after 10 seconds. The client retries with an idempotency key — the retry goes to a different matching service instance. The ride_id is already created in PostgreSQL so the retry is idempotent. No duplicate rides are created."

### Scoring Your Performance

| Dimension | What to check |
|-----------|---------------|
| Requirements | Did you ask about scale, latency, availability, consistency? |
| API Design | Correct verbs? Versioned? Paginated lists? |
| Data Model | Correct DB types? Key schemas defined? |
| Infrastructure | All 5 layers? Both read and write paths? |
| Optimization | At least 2 bottlenecks with solutions AND trade-offs? |

### Common Mistakes in Mocks

1. **Not narrating while drawing**. Silence during diagramming makes the interviewer think you're stuck.
2. **Treating the mock as practice you'll review later**. Treat every mock as the real thing — simulate the pressure.
3. **Not reviewing the recording**. If possible, record yourself and watch it back. You will notice verbal tics, pauses, and missed phases that you didn't notice in the moment.
keyTakeaways: The mock interview is your most important practice — treat it as the real thing
Score yourself on all 5 RADIO dimensions after every mock
Narrate while drawing — silence signals confusion
Trade-offs are the most common underscored dimension — state them proactively

quizBank.question: What does RADIO stand for in the system design interview framework?
options: A) Research, Analysis, Design, Implementation, Operations
B) Requirements, API, Data, Infrastructure, Optimization
C) Requirements, Architecture, Diagram, Integration, Outcomes
D) Research, API, Database, Infrastructure, Observations
quizBank.explanation: RADIO = Requirements (clarify scope and scale), API (design the interface), Data (choose database and schema), Infrastructure (draw the architecture), Optimization (address bottlenecks).

quizBank.question: In a 45-minute system design interview, how many minutes should you spend on the Infrastructure (architecture diagram) phase?
options: A) 5 minutes
B) 10 minutes
C) 20 minutes
D) 35 minutes
quizBank.explanation: The time budget is 5/5/5/20/10: 5 min Requirements, 5 min API, 5 min Data, 20 min Infrastructure, 10 min Optimization. Infrastructure gets the largest block because it is the highest-weighted phase.

quizBank.question: Which of the following is a NON-functional requirement?
options: A) Users can post tweets up to 280 characters
B) The system must support 300 million DAU
C) Users can follow other users
D) Users can see a timeline of recent tweets
quizBank.explanation: Non-functional requirements describe quality attributes (scale, latency, availability, consistency) rather than user-visible features. 300M DAU is a scale requirement, not a feature.

quizBank.question: What HTTP status code should a URL shortener return when redirecting a user from the short URL to the original URL?
options: A) 200 OK
B) 201 Created
C) 301 Moved Permanently
D) 404 Not Found
quizBank.explanation: 301 Moved Permanently is the correct redirect code for a permanent URL mapping. The browser caches this and will not hit the shortener again for future requests to that short URL. Use 302 if you want to track every click (no browser caching).

quizBank.question: You need to store 500 billion social graph edges with sub-millisecond multi-hop traversal queries. Which database do you choose?
options: A) PostgreSQL with self-joins
B) MongoDB with embedded documents
C) Neo4j or a graph database
D) Cassandra with a composite partition key
quizBank.explanation: Graph databases (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune) are designed for multi-hop traversals (friends-of-friends, shortest path). SQL self-joins become O(n^k) for k hops — infeasible at 500B edges. Cassandra and MongoDB do not support graph traversals natively.

quizBank.question: What pagination type should you use for an infinite-scroll social feed and why?
options: A) Offset pagination — simplest to implement
B) Cursor-based pagination — stable under inserts and O(1) at any position
C) Keyset pagination — best for financial reports
D) Page-number pagination — most familiar to users
quizBank.explanation: Cursor-based pagination uses an opaque token (typically encoding the last-seen ID + timestamp) to fetch the next page. It is stable when new items are inserted (offset pagination would skip items) and is O(1) regardless of cursor depth.

quizBank.question: A database primary server goes down. What is the standard recovery sequence?
options: A) Restart the primary server and replay the WAL log
B) Health check detects failure → promote a replica to primary → retry queued writes
C) Delete the failed node and provision a new one from a backup
D) Route all traffic to the cache until the primary recovers
quizBank.explanation: Standard HA database recovery: health checks detect the failure, a read replica is promoted to primary (automatic in multi-AZ managed DBs), and queued writes are retried. This takes 30–120 seconds. Routing to cache only works for reads, not writes.

quizBank.question: What is the circuit breaker pattern used for?
options: A) Preventing SQL injection attacks
B) Stopping cascading failures by rejecting requests to a failing service until it recovers
C) Balancing load across multiple servers
D) Encrypting traffic between microservices
quizBank.explanation: A circuit breaker monitors failure rates for calls to a downstream service. When failures exceed a threshold, it 'opens' — all subsequent calls fail fast without hitting the service. After a timeout, it enters 'half-open' state and allows one test request. Success closes the breaker; failure reopens it.

quizBank.question: What is the correct REST URL for retrieving all tweets posted by a specific user?
options: A) GET /getUserTweets?userId=123
B) GET /tweets?user=123
C) GET /users/123/tweets
D) POST /users/getTweets
quizBank.explanation: REST convention: use nouns in hierarchical resource paths. /users/123/tweets clearly expresses 'tweets belonging to user 123'. Verbs in URLs (/getUserTweets) violate REST principles. Query parameters can filter but should not identify the primary resource.

quizBank.question: When should you use gRPC instead of REST for service-to-service communication?
options: A) When the service is accessed by web browsers
B) When low latency, strong typing, and streaming are required for internal microservices
C) When the API must be publicly documented with OpenAPI
D) When the payload is primarily HTML
quizBank.explanation: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (binary encoding, ~5× smaller than JSON), HTTP/2 (multiplexed streams), and strong typed contracts (proto files). These characteristics make it ideal for internal service calls where latency matters and you control both client and server.

quizBank.question: A system has 100K read QPS and 5K write QPS on a single PostgreSQL instance. Which scaling action addresses the bottleneck?
options: A) Add more application servers
B) Shard the database by user_id
C) Add Redis cache and read replicas to offload the 100K reads
D) Switch to MongoDB for its flexible schema
quizBank.explanation: PostgreSQL handles ~5K QPS — so 5K write QPS is at the limit but manageable. The 100K read QPS is the bottleneck. Solution: Redis cache (absorbs 80-95% of reads) + read replicas (handle remaining reads). Sharding addresses write QPS, not read QPS.

quizBank.question: What is the trade-off introduced by sharding a database by user_id?
options: A) Data is no longer durable
B) Cross-shard queries (e.g., global trending) become expensive scatter-gather operations
C) Individual user queries become slower
D) The database can no longer use indexes
quizBank.explanation: Sharding by user_id means all data for a user is on one shard (great for user-centric queries). But queries that cross shard boundaries (trending topics, global analytics) must query all shards and merge results — a scatter-gather that is O(num_shards) in latency.

quizBank.question: How do you handle a client submitting the same write request twice due to a network retry?
options: A) Accept both requests and store duplicates
B) Client sends an idempotency key; server checks Redis before processing and returns cached result if key exists
C) Block all retries with rate limiting
D) Use a UNIQUE constraint on all fields
quizBank.explanation: Idempotency keys (UUIDs generated by the client) make write operations safe to retry. The server stores processed keys in Redis with a TTL. On receiving a duplicate key, it returns the original response without reprocessing — achieving exactly-once semantics.

quizBank.question: What is the polyglot persistence pattern?
options: A) Using the same database for all data to simplify operations
B) Using multiple different database types optimised for different access patterns within the same system
C) Storing data in multiple languages
D) Replicating data across multiple data centers
quizBank.explanation: Polyglot persistence: e.g., PostgreSQL for user accounts (ACID), Cassandra for events (write-heavy), Redis for cache (fast reads), Elasticsearch for search (full-text). Each database is chosen to fit its specific access pattern rather than forcing one database to do everything.

quizBank.question: Why should you always announce phase transitions during a system design interview?
options: A) It gives the interviewer a chance to stop you
B) It demonstrates structured thinking, keeps the interviewer oriented, and signals time management
C) It is required by interview etiquette
D) It allows you to reset the clock for each phase
quizBank.explanation: Interviewers score structured communication as a named dimension. Announcing transitions ('Let me move on to the API design') shows you have a plan, keeps the interviewer tracking your progress, and demonstrates time awareness — all of which directly improve your score.

quizBank.question: A ride-sharing app needs to find all available drivers within 5 km of a rider's location in under 10ms. What is the most appropriate technology?
options: A) PostgreSQL with PostGIS extension and a spatial index
B) Redis GEORADIUS command on an in-memory geospatial set
C) Elasticsearch with a geo_distance query
D) Cassandra with a composite key of (grid_cell, driver_id)
quizBank.explanation: Redis GEO commands (GEORADIUS, GEOSEARCH) run in O(N + log N) on an in-memory sorted set. For 5M active drivers queried at high frequency, Redis satisfies the sub-10ms latency requirement. PostgreSQL/PostGIS and Elasticsearch can also do this but with higher latency from disk I/O.

quizBank.question: What is the Saga pattern used for in microservices?
options: A) Routing requests to the correct microservice
B) Maintaining data consistency across multiple services without a distributed transaction
C) Monitoring service health across a cluster
D) Caching responses across service boundaries
quizBank.explanation: The Saga pattern manages multi-step transactions across services without two-phase commit. Each step publishes an event on success; if a step fails, compensating transactions undo the previous steps. This provides eventual consistency without the distributed lock overhead of 2PC.

quizBank.question: Which phase of RADIO is most commonly run short by candidates, and why does this hurt their score?
options: A) Requirements — running it short means they miss the scale numbers
B) Optimization — running it short means no scaling discussion, which is heavily weighted
C) API Design — APIs are not scored in most interviews
D) Data Model — interviewers do not care about database choice
quizBank.explanation: Candidates who over-invest in requirements and architecture draw often reach minute 40 with no scaling discussion. The Optimization phase (bottlenecks, trade-offs) is heavily weighted because it tests engineering depth. Skipping it leaves significant scoring on the table.

quizBank.question: Why is it better to use cursor-based pagination than offset pagination for a social media timeline?
options: A) Cursor pagination is faster to implement
B) Offset pagination skips or duplicates items when new content is inserted between pages
C) Cursor pagination works with any database type
D) Offset pagination does not support filtering
quizBank.explanation: With offset pagination, if 5 new tweets are inserted at the top of the feed between page 1 and page 2 requests, page 2 will repeat 5 tweets the user already saw. Cursor pagination uses a pointer to the last-seen item — new inserts do not affect subsequent page requests.

quizBank.question: What is the correct order of concern when selecting a database in a system design interview?
options: A) Choose your favourite → justify later
B) Choose the most popular → it will probably work
C) Access pattern first → scale requirements second → then select database type
D) Scale first → consistency model second → then select database type
quizBank.explanation: Database selection should start with access pattern (what queries will you run?), then scale (how many rows, what QPS?). Access pattern determines the database category (relational, wide-column, graph, etc.); scale determines whether you need sharding or managed cloud services.

interviewTips: Open with: 'I'll use a structured approach — let me start with requirements before I jump to design' — this immediately signals maturity
State the framework at the start: 'I'll cover requirements, API, data model, architecture, and then scaling' — then execute it
Announce every phase transition aloud: 'I have enough context — let me move to the API design'
Draw while narrating: never draw in silence — interviewers need to track your thinking
State trade-offs unprompted: 'The trade-off here is...' — candidates who wait to be asked score lower
When challenged on a design choice, acknowledge the alternative before defending yours: 'DynamoDB would also work here — I chose Cassandra because...'
When you don't know something, say so: 'I don't know the exact mechanism — my hypothesis is X, I'd validate it by...'
Always ask about consistency vs availability: it changes the database choice and the architecture significantly
Watch the clock yourself — it is your responsibility to manage time, not the interviewer's
The wrap-up (last 5 minutes) is free points: summarise trade-offs and ask if the interviewer wants to explore any area deeper
commonMistakes: Jumping to infrastructure before clarifying requirements — you may design the wrong system confidently
Spending more than 5–7 minutes on requirements — over-spending here leaves no time for architecture and scaling
Drawing the architecture in silence — interviewers cannot score thinking they cannot observe
Not mentioning trade-offs unprompted — saying 'there are no trade-offs' or waiting to be asked signals shallow understanding
Treating the database selection as a coin flip — always justify with access pattern and scale requirements
